OFFICE OF

THE CITY ATTORNEY
CITY OF SAN DIEGO

CITY ADMINISTRATION BUILDING
202 "C" STREET

RONALD L JOHNSON
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

JOHN W. WITT

SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101-3863

STUART H. SPETT
SENIOR CHIEF DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEY

CITY ATTORNEY

TELEPHONE (619) 236-6220

JACK KATZ
SENIOR CHIEF DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEY

FAX I (619) 236-7215

CURTIS M. FITZPATRICK
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY
SPECIAL PROJECTS

March 27, 1991


Ms. DeAnne Stone, Staff Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Stone:

Request for Assistance

As a follow up to our telephone conversation of March 13,
1991, I enclose a memorandum from the City of San Diego's Auditor
and Comptroller Ed Ryan requesting clarification of the new
legislation and rules concerning gifts and honoraria. Based on
our telephone conversation, it is my understanding that Mr. Ryan,
an appointed official, does not have to disclose reimbursement
from the state or federal government as a gift or honorarium.
Also, Mr. Ryan is not disqualified from sitting on a federal or
state board because of a financial conflict of interest arising
from the reimbursements.

On behalf of Mr. Ryan, I am hereby requesting written
confirmation of the above information. Background information is
contained in the attached memorandum from the City Auditor and
Comptroller to me dated March 14, 1991. Please call me if you
need further information.

On behalf of Mr. Ryan and myself, thank you for your
attention to this matter.

Sincerely yours,

JOHN W. WITT, City Attorney

By [illegible]
Cristie C. McGuire
Deputy City Attorney

CCM:jrl:
Attachment
cc Ed Ryan, City Auditor and Comptroller

City of San Diego

MEMORANDUM


DATE:           March 14, 1991

TO:             Cristie C. McGuire, City Attorney's Office

FROM:           City Auditor and Comptroller

SUBJECT:        Rules Concerning 1991 Honorarium

_________________________________________________________________

As we discussed, in filling out my Conflict of Interest forms for
calendar 1990, I am concerned about a prospective circumstance in
1991. My circumstance prospectively will be as follows:

1.  I am presently and have been for the last 14 years a member of
    the State Board of Accountancy Qualifications Committee. The
    State of California reimburses my expenses and pays me a per
    diem. The per diem is deposited into a City of San Diego fund
    and the expense reimbursement partially offsets my expenses.

    It is my understanding that the new rules do not require my
    disclosing of travel reimbursement or the per diem. Is that
    correct?

2.  During 1991, I have been appointed to a new U.S. Comptroller
    General Government Auditing Standards Advisory Council. They
    will be meeting quarterly in Washington, D.C. The Federal
    Government through the U.S. Comptroller General's Office, will
    be reimbursing my travel expenses. How do I need to report
    this reimbursement? Does this reporting cause me any problems
    either being a city official or a member of the State Board of
    Accountancy Qualifications Committee under the new State
    rules?

    My question deals with New Honoraria Prohibition instructions
    that say; members of State Boards and Commissions, and
    designated state employees may not accept an honorarium from
    any source if a member or employee will be required to
    report the receipt of income on the statement of Economic
    Interests.    The instructions on Appendix III Honoraria
    indicate; payment or reimbursement for travel in connection
    with an event is reportable as an honorarium, if the travel
    was outside of California. The next paragraph also says;
    payment must be reported as a gift or income unless it is from
    a state, local or federal government agency.      The next
    paragraph goes on to say; an honorarium from a reportable
    source must be disclosed on Schedule F or Schedule D.

Cristie C. McGuire
March 14, 1991
Page 2


It is my hope that all of that means that as long as my
reimbursement is from the state or federal government, even if the
travel is outside of California, I do not have to report it as
honorarium, and I do not fall under any of the prohibitions on
honorarium for either local or state purposes.

Your assistance in verifying the accuracy of this assumption is
greatly appreciated.

Ed Ryan
City Auditor and Comptroller

ER:mem
Enclosure
